posts.text:
To stop that trend, gotta re-learn the lost art of “taking positions that voters agree with” on issues that don’t have tons of technical content.

nytimes.com/2026/03/16/opini…

New Van Hollen tax plan with twenty co-sponsors vs 1993 Dem tax plan — upscale liberals have developed class consciousness and are now threatening to drive fiscal policy. 

slowboring.com/p/upscale-lib…

Bill Clinton’s super moderate cultural politics allowed for an aggressive approach to revenue that is inconceivable for today’s Democrats 

slowboring.com/p/upscale-lib…

Democrats have developed an upscale voting base and they are starting to develop tax policy priorities to match it.

Wonks who don’t like this (most of them!) need to join the fight for cultural moderation so we can avert this drift.

slowboring.com/p/upscale-lib…

Being tougher on crime, more skeptical of the trans activist position on youth transition and sports, and more open to fossil fuels all also help but by lesser amounts. 

Republicans can also win by moderating on LGB employment discrimination, health care, and the minimum wage.

But normally moving to the center wins votes. 

The two biggest winning moderations are on racial preferences in college admissions & small business support.

Merit pay for teachers is big, so is LGB content in school and welcoming fewer asylum claims.

nytimes.com/2026/03/16/opini…

First a win for progressives — their research shows that there are some issues like social security, skilled immigration, and the minimum wage where the conventional Dem view is so popular that moving to the center backfires.

Today in the New York Times with the help of state of the art political science, I tackle the question “who should Democrats throw under the bus to win?”

They did a large experiment to show which policy moderations move the most voters.

nytimes.com/2026/03/16/opini…
Strait of Hormuz winning the Oscar for “best economic chokepoint” was predictable but deserved.
It’s crazy that Frankenstein got so many nominations … did people not notice that the movie was bad?
Getting jitters waiting for the oil futures markets to resume trading.

Movies I like a lot winning Oscars makes me feel old. 

Should give it to CODA (remember that one???) every year. 

slowboring.com/p/could-the-s…

DC Sierra Club says McDuffie is bad on energy affordability and cited as evidence his opposition to a bill that has nothing to do with energy, a cost-shift bill that raises average costs, and a bill that would massively increase construction costs.

There are all these “why are people nostalgic for the 90s” takes floating around this week and isn’t nostalgia just literally always focused on the period 30 years ago? 

In related news, I got my son to watch “Broken Arrow” and “Goldeneye” with me this weekend.

It’s logistically difficult to get around Sudan and as a result nobody really even has casualty estimates 

amp.dw.com/en/civil-war-in-s…

To me, the "democratic" part of this story was when elected officials decided they wanted to make a policy change to facilitate rooftop solar. 

The gauntlet of talking to people at meetings was neither democratic nor deliberative. 

slowboring.com/p/community-m…

No, the abundance crowd believes (accurately) that the community meeting process empowers a wildly unrepresentative group of people to exert disproportionate influence over the political system.


Daniel Steinmetz-Jenkins (@daniel_dsj2110)

Habermas's deliberative democracy stands in contrast with the Abundance crowd who sees democratic deliberation as another red tape that gets in the way of technocratic urban renewal projects



— https://nitter.net/daniel_dsj2110/status/2032892874061177062#m

Underrated issue here, but one of the main impacts of all the ICE hiring bonuses is to induce cops to leave local police departments.  

washingtonpost.com/dc-md-va/…

We obviously do not have the exact architecture of Split,  but plenty of East Coast beaches have tall buildings near them — it's a peculiar conceit of the California Mind that highly in-demand real estate shouldn't be developed.


Hayden (@the_transit_guy)

I find it statistically impossible to believe that in a “rich” country of 330 million people, there’s no demand for a single coastal city/town that looks like this.



— https://nitter.net/the_transit_guy/status/2033157213645050204#m

Today in the NYT, I discuss some fresh research on which moderations move the most voters.

www.nytimes.com/2026/03/16/o...
This is an amazing platform

If per person spending on the elderly goes up and the elderly share of the population also goes up, everything else gets squeezed and everyone is mad.

But it’s what the voters say they want.

www.slowboring.com/p/the-demoni...

A defense of the unfairly maligned “corporate landlord” — there’s no reason rental housing should be all owned by small-time landlords, this isn’t like the local coffee shop that’s a treasured neighborhood resource.

www.slowboring.com/p/maybe-all-...
When I wrote down "valley" as a potential story idea, what do you think I meant?

Successful military adventures like the recent one in Venezuela beget hubris and set the stage for new fiascos as arrogant leaders forget that you can’t just count on getting lucky all the time.

www.slowboring.com/p/nobody-pla...

“Stop AOC” is a terrible mission for 2028

www.slowboring.com/p/aoc-is-not...

There are levels of NIMBYism where even detached single family homes aren’t acceptable 

www.bangordailynews.com/2026/03/05/h...

From 50,000 feet I think it's easy to post on the internet "we need to return to real state capacity like in the New Deal instead of this endless reliance on contracting and outsourcing." 

But contracting is a very convenient workaround to public sector pay compression, unions, job tenure, etc
Very good points here, though I question whether progressives are prepared to really do what it would take to reduce dependence on these kind of contractors.

Want to get out ahead of this and concede that Yglesias Thought may be totally discredited as it turns out that "don't change anything, hope Trump just fucks up spectacularly in a way that sinks Republicans" works fine as a plan.
Platner dining table is 25% off this week

It’s very unpleasant to be subjected to an insane internet pile-on, but I think many writers end up sabotaging themselves by trying too hard to avoid the inevitable.

www.slowboring.com/p/everyone-g...
Not sure these guys have thought this through

War unpopular, Trump blamed for high prices, Democrats have a nice lead in generic ballot, Republicans still more trusted on immigration.

www.nbcnews.com/politics/202...
Thought I’d slept late this morning but it turns out Big Government changed time overnight.
Trump’s gotta incorporate AI into his workflow next time
Education results have gotten worse during a period of time when we’ve decided to try less hard to teach kids
Upscale liberals are developing class consciousness (by Matthew Yglesias)
Sunday Thread + Mailbag (by Halina Bennet)
Saturday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
Could the Second Mexican Empire have endured? (by Matthew Yglesias). Plus the case for proportional representation, Obama’s foreign policy, and an S-Bahn for Greater Boston
I don’t buy your “dating recession” (by Halina Bennet). The collapse of romantic infrastructure is an engineering failure, not a Gen Z character flaw.
The demonic policy strangling the British economy  (by Matthew Yglesias)
Thursday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
Maybe *all* rental housing should be owned by large institutional investors (by Matthew Yglesias)

One bad provision could sink a critical bipartisan housing bill  (by Halina Bennet). Despite sweeping support and real substance, a poorly drafted investor ban could kill the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act.
Nobody plans for a quagmire  (by Matthew Yglesias). Behind every military disaster is an honest mistake.
Tuesday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
A.O.C. is not the problem (by Matthew Yglesias). Centrist Democrats need to push for change, not bandwagon behind the broken establishment.
First primaries, first lessons (by Halina Bennet). Plus, more Republican retirements and Trump’s Iran problem
Everyone gets canceled sooner or later (by Matthew Yglesias)
Sunday Thread + Mailbag (by Halina Bennet)
